ground_truth.title: Electrical Wiring: Components, Types & Safety Basics
ground_truth.author: Ross Aresco
ground_truth.main_content:
Electrical wiring refers to the installation of cabling and associated devices such as switches, distribution panels, outlets, and light fittings within a structure. It is essential to every electrical system, ensuring safe and reliable distribution of power across circuits. Electrical wiring is subject to safety regulations for design and installation, which affect the types and sizes of the cables you can use.

Electricity enters your home through a service head from a series of outdoor power lines or an underground connection. A typical service head consists of two 120-volt wires and one neutral wire that supply power to lights and appliances in your home. The 120-volt circuits use one phase of the electrical service to power standard home appliances. Larger appliances like water heaters and clothes dryers require a 240-volt circuit, which is created using both 120-volt wires and the neutral wire. The electrical service panel is the central distribution point for delivering electricity to switches, outlets, and appliances throughout your house.

Key electrical terms include: Volts (electromotive force), Watt (unit of power), kW (1000 watts), kWh (unit of energy), and MCB (miniature circuit breaker).

Electrical Wire vs. Electrical Cable: Electrical cables assemble two or more wires in a single jacket. Wires are the individual insulated or bare conductors inside the jacket. Wires come in different sizes to work with the amperage of the circuit in which they're used. The larger the number, the smaller the wire. The most common gauge sizes for residential work are 14-gauge and 12-gauge.

Wire Color Codes: Black and red wires indicate hot wires carrying live current. White and gray wires indicate neutral-charged wires. White wires with black or red tape are hot wires. Green wires and bare copper are used for grounding. Blue and yellow wires are sometimes used as hot wires, with blue commonly used for travelers in three-way switch applications.

Most modern homes use nonmetallic (NM) cable that consists of two or more wires wrapped inside colored sheathing. The package of wires usually contains one or more hot wires plus a neutral wire and a ground wire.

Safety Tips: Have the proper tools including a multimeter and combination sheath/wire stripper. Know which colored wire goes where to avoid electrical shock. Leave wires long enough to protrude at least 3 inches from the box. Use quality switches and outlets. Check voltage before touching wires.

Common Mistakes: Never connect wires outside electrical boxes (junction boxes protect connections from damage and contain sparks). Leave wires at least 3 inches outside the box. Protect plastic-sheathed cable that is left exposed between framing members.

Common Household Issues: Circuit breaker trips indicate problems. Electricity surges, sags, or dips can damage electronics. Scarce or dead outlets pose dangers and should be replaced by an electrician.
with: Electrical wiring refers to the installation of cabling and associated devices such as switches, distribution panels, outlets, and light fittings within a structure.
The electrical service panel is the central distribution point for delivering electricity to switches, outlets, and appliances throughout your house.
Black, red, and white wires with black or red tape will always indicate a hot wire. Hot means the wire carries a live current from your electric panel to the destination.
Green insulated wires are often used for grounding. Green wires should not be used for any purpose other than grounding.
Never connect wires outside electrical boxes. Junction boxes protect the connections from accidental damage and contain sparks and heat from a loose connection or short circuit.
